original_abstract:
Elemental compositions of organic aerosol (OA) particles, such as oxygen-to-carbon (O:C) and hydrogen-to-carbon (H:C) ratios, provide critical constraints on aerosol sources, chemical evolution, and environmental impacts. The Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) is the primary tool for these measurements, utilizing empirical calibrations (Aiken et al., 2008) to account for ion fragmentation. This study evaluates these methods using an expanded laboratory dataset of multifunctional organic standards representative of highly oxidized ambient OA. 

Evaluation of the "Aiken-Explicit" method, which utilizes measured intensities for all ions, demonstrates high accuracy, reproducing O:C and H:C ratios within 20% and 12%, respectively. However, the more standard "Aiken-Ambient" method—which estimates H₂O⁺ and CO⁺ to avoid air interferences—systematically underestimates these ratios, particularly for alcohols and diacids. Combined AMS and vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) ionization measurements reveal that these biases stem from the significant thermal decomposition (dehydration and decarboxylation) of oxidized species on the AMS vaporizer, even at temperatures as low as 200 °C.

To address these systematic errors, we introduce an "Improved-Ambient" elemental analysis method. By using specific ion fragments as markers (e.g., $f_{CO2}$ and $f_{CHO}$) to correct for molecular functionality-dependent biases, this method reproduces known O:C and H:C ratios of individual standards within 28% and 13%, respectively. Accuracy further improves for complex mixtures typical of atmospheric conditions. Applying this method to ambient data results in O:C and H:C values that are 27% and 11% higher, respectively, than previous estimates, suggesting that ambient OA is more oxygenated than previously recognized. In contrast, the carbon oxidation state ($OS_C$) remains a robust metric across methods, as it is largely unaffected by hydration or dehydration processes. These results provide an updated framework for interpreting OA chemical spectra and secondary organic aerosol evolution.
humanized_abstract:
The oxygen-to-carbon (O:C) and hydrogen-to-carbon (H:C) ratios are elemental compositions that carry significant information about aerosol sources, processes, and effects. These variables are measured using the Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) via empirical calibrations put forth by Aiken et al. (2008). This study uses a significantly larger laboratory dataset consisting of multifunctional organic compounds (MOCs) analogous to highly oxidized organic aerosol (OA) found in the atmosphere.

We have investigated the accuracy of measuring O:C and H:C ratios of oxidized organics in two ways. First, we assess the performance of what we label the “Aiken-Explicit” method, which uses measured intensities for all ions: this achieves excellent accuracy for O:C and H:C ratios with average relative errors less than 20 and 12 % respectively. Secondly, we explore the “Aiken-Ambient” method (more frequently used) that approximates the intensity of H₂O⁺, and CO⁺, species to mitigate interference from ambient air: these approximations lead to systematic biases in the measured ratios of H:C and O:C (up to a factor of 2) towards lower values for alcohol and diacid species. Additional information on composition and thermal stability of these species provided by combined AMS-VUV analysis shows that the lower values of these ratios are a direct consequence of severe dehydration and decarboxylation occurring on the AMS vaporizer, which is estimated to occur at a temperature as low as 200 °C.

To correct for the systematic errors in the ambient environment, we introduce the Improved-Ambient (IA) elemental analysis method. Using the markers $f_{CO2}$ and $f_{CHO}$ we correct for the biases associated with different molecular functionalities and achieve an accuracy of within a factor of 1.28 and 1.13 for O:C and H:C ratios of known individuals standards. Such accuracy is achieved for complex mixtures that mimic more closely the atmospheric conditions. As illustrated in Figure 1, the ambient OA is found to have O:C and H:C ratios 1.27 and 1.11 times larger than previous measurements. The carbon oxidation state $OS_C$ is proven to be a robust quantity, as it is not affected by the hydration/dehydration issues. Overall the IA method provides a new insight into the IAA, S/N OA chemical spectra as well as in the secondary organic aerosol (SOA) evolution.

document.output:

document.input:
Elemental compositions of organic aerosol (OA) particles, such as oxygen-to-carbon (O:C) and hydrogen-to-carbon (H:C) ratios, provide critical constraints on aerosol sources, chemical evolution, and environmental impacts. The Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) is the primary tool for these measurements, utilizing empirical calibrations (Aiken et al., 2008) to account for ion fragmentation. This study evaluates these methods using an expanded laboratory dataset of multifunctional organic standards representative of highly oxidized ambient OA. 

Evaluation of the "Aiken-Explicit" method, which utilizes measured intensities for all ions, demonstrates high accuracy, reproducing O:C and H:C ratios within 20% and 12%, respectively. However, the more standard "Aiken-Ambient" method—which estimates H₂O⁺ and CO⁺ to avoid air interferences—systematically underestimates these ratios, particularly for alcohols and diacids. Combined AMS and vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) ionization measurements reveal that these biases stem from the significant thermal decomposition (dehydration and decarboxylation) of oxidized species on the AMS vaporizer, even at temperatures as low as 200 °C.

To address these systematic errors, we introduce an "Improved-Ambient" elemental analysis method. By using specific ion fragments as markers (e.g., $f_{CO2}$ and $f_{CHO}$) to correct for molecular functionality-dependent biases, this method reproduces known O:C and H:C ratios of individual standards within 28% and 13%, respectively. Accuracy further improves for complex mixtures typical of atmospheric conditions. Applying this method to ambient data results in O:C and H:C values that are 27% and 11% higher, respectively, than previous estimates, suggesting that ambient OA is more oxygenated than previously recognized. In contrast, the carbon oxidation state ($OS_C$) remains a robust metric across methods, as it is largely unaffected by hydration or dehydration processes. These results provide an updated framework for interpreting OA chemical spectra and secondary organic aerosol evolution.
